sentences.text: This appendix contains the rationale for RISC-V ISA extensions at the time they were ratified.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions

sentences.text: Unlike the ISA specification, this appendix is ordered chronologically, so as to convey the motivation and architectural reasoning underpinning each extension at the time of ratification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions

sentences.text: For extensions ratified prior to the conception of this appendix (ca. 2025), the rationale will be added over time.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions

sentences.text: In cases where the rationale was not recorded, the authors and editors will synthesize it from the historical record.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions

sentences.text: Since a CSR for security and / or global PMP behavior settings is not available with the current spec, we needed to define a new `mseccfg` CSR.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: This new CSR will allow us to add further security configuration options in the future and also allow developers to verify the existence of the new mechanisms defined on this extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: There are use cases where developers want to enforce PMP rules in M-mode during the boot process, that are also able to modify, merge, and / or remove later on.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: Since a rule that is enforced in M-mode also needs to be locked (or else badly written or malicious M-mode software can remove it at any time), the only way for developers to approach this is to keep adding PMP rules to the chain and rely on rule priority.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: This is a waste of PMP rules and since it’s only needed during boot, ``mseccfg.RLB`` is a simple workaround that can be used temporarily and then disabled and locked down. + Also when ``mseccfg.MML`` is set, according to 4b it’s not possible to add a Shared-Region rule with executable privileges.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: So RLB can be set temporarily during the boot process to register such regions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: Note that it’s still possible to register executable Shared-Region rules using initial register settings (that may include ``mseccfg.MML`` being set and the rule being set on PMP registers) on *PMP reset*, without using RLB. + [WARNING] *Be aware that RLB introduces a security vulnerability if left set after the boot process is over and in general it should be used with caution, even when used temporarily.* Having editable PMP rules in M-mode gives a false sense of security since it only takes a few malicious instructions to lift any PMP restrictions this way.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: It doesn’t make sense to have a security control in place and leave it unprotected.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: Rule Locking Bypass is only meant as a way to optimize the allocation of PMP rules, catch errors during debugging, and allow the bootrom/firmware to register executable Shared-Region rules.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: If developers / vendors have no use for such functionality, they should never set ``mseccfg.RLB`` and if possible hard-wire it to 0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: In any case *RLB should be disabled and locked as soon as possible*. + + [IMPORTANT] Since PMP rules with a higher priority override rules with a lower priority, locked rules must precede non-locked rules.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: With the current spec M-mode can access any memory region unless restricted by a PMP rule with the ``pmpcfg.L`` bit set.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: There are cases where this approach is overly permissive, and although it’s possible to restrict M-mode by adding PMP rules during the boot process, this can also be seen as a waste of PMP rules.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: Having the option to block anything by default, and use PMP as an allowlist for M-mode is considered a safer approach.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: This functionality may be used during the boot process or upon *PMP reset*, using initial register settings. +
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: The current dual meaning of the ``pmpcfg.L`` bit that marks a rule as Locked and *enforced* on all modes is neither flexible nor clean.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: With the introduction of Machine Mode Lock-down the ``pmpcfg.L`` bit distinguishes between rules that are *enforced* *only* in M-mode (M-mode-only) or *only* in S/U-modes (S/U-mode-only).
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: The rule locking becomes part of the definition of an M-mode-only rule, since when a rule is added in M mode, if not locked, can be modified or removed in a few instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: On the other hand, S/U modes can’t modify PMP rules anyway so locking them doesn’t make sense.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: This separation between M-mode-only and S/U-mode-only rules also allows us to distinguish which regions are to be used by processes in Machine mode (``pmpcfg.L 1``) and which by Supervisor or User mode processes (``pmpcfg.L 0``), in the same way the U bit on the Virtual Memory’s PTEs marks which Virtual Memory pages are to be used by User mode applications (U=1) and which by the Supervisor / OS (U=0).
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: With this distinction in place we are able to implement memory access and execution prevention in M-mode for any physical memory region that is not M-mode-only. + An attacker that manages to tamper with a memory region used by S/U mode, even after successfully tricking a process running in M-mode to use or execute that region, will fail to perform a successful attack since that region will be S/U-mode-only hence any access when in M-mode will trigger an access exception. + +
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: + Shared-region rules can be used both for zero-copy data transfers and for sharing code segments.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: The latter may be used for example to allow S/U-mode to execute code by the vendor, that makes use of some vendor-specific ISA extension, without having to go through the firmware with an ecall.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: This is similar to the vDSO approach followed on Linux, that allows user space code to execute kernel code without having to perform a system call. + To make sure that shared data regions can’t be executed and shared code regions can’t be modified, the encoding changes the meaning of the ``pmpcfg.X bit``.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: In case of shared data regions, with the exception of the ``pmpcfg.LRWX=1111`` encoding, the ``pmpcfg.X`` bit marks the capability of S/U-mode to write to that region, so it’s not possible to encode an executable shared data region.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: In case of shared code regions, the ``pmpcfg.X`` bit marks the capability of M-mode to read from that region, and since ``pmpcfg.RW=01`` is used for encoding the shared region, it’s not possible to encode a shared writable code region. + + + ..
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: The idea with this restriction is that after the Firmware or the OS running in M-mode is initialized and ``mseccfg.MML`` is set, no new code regions are expected to be added since nothing else is expected to run in M-mode (everything else will run in S/U mode).
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: Since we want to limit the attack surface of the system as much as possible, it makes sense to disallow any new code regions which may include malicious code, to be added/executed in M-mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: In case ``mseccfg.MMWP`` is not set, M-mode can still access and execute any region not covered by a PMP rule.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: Since we try to prevent M-mode from executing malicious code and since an attacker may manage to place code on some region not covered by PMP (e.g. a directly-addressable flash memory), we need to ensure that M-mode can only execute the code segments initialized during firmware / OS initialization.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

sentences.text: We are only using the encoding ``pmpcfg.RW=01`` together with ``mseccfg.MML``, if ``mseccfg.MML`` is not set the encoding remains usable for future use.
sentences.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode
